title: Logging & Observability
description: Python logging from basics to Staff+ FAANG depth: structured logging, OpenTelemetry, correlation IDs, PII redaction, sampling, log aggregation, and observability strategy at scale.

sections.title: 1. Logging Fundamentals — The logging Module
sections.description:
Python's logging module has 5 levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL. Configure through code or logging.config.dictConfig().

Logger hierarchy: Loggers are named by dotted path (e.g., myapp.service.api). Child loggers propagate to parents by default. Configure the root logger once; child loggers inherit settings.

Never use print() for logging. print() goes to stdout, can't be filtered by severity, has no timestamps, and doesn't support structured output.

FAANG angle: 'How do you add logging to a Python microservice?' — Use logging.getLogger(__name__) at module level. Configure via dictConfig(). Use structured logs (JSON) for machine parsing. Always log at ERROR level for operational issues, WARNING for degraded state, INFO for normal operations, DEBUG for troubleshooting (off by default in production).

sections.title: 2. Structured Logging with JSON
sections.description:
Structured logs emit key-value pairs (JSON), not freeform strings. Machines parse them; humans can still read them. Every production Python service at FAANG uses structured logging.

python-json-logger formats log records as JSON. structlog provides composable processors for richer structured output.

Extra fields: Pass contextual data via the extra parameter or use LoggerAdapters for thread-safe context.

FAANG angle: 'Design the logging format for a new microservice' — Always include: timestamp (ISO 8601), severity, logger name, message, request_id, trace_id, service_name, environment, version, duration_ms, and any domain-specific fields. All fields in lowercase snake_case.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Structured logging with python-json-logger ===
# pip install python-json-logger
from pythonjsonlogger import jsonlogger

handler = logging.StreamHandler()
formatter = jsonlogger.JsonFormatter(
    fmt='%(asctime)s %(name)s %(levelname)s %(message)s %(request_id)s',
    datefmt='%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S%z',
)
handler.setFormatter(formatter)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

logger.info('request completed', extra={'request_id': 'abc123', 'duration_ms': 45})
# Output: {"asctime": "2026-06-23T10:30:00+0000", "name": "__main__", "levelname": "INFO", "message": "request completed", "request_id": "abc123", "duration_ms": 45}

# === structlog for composable structured logging ===
# pip install structlog
import structlog

structlog.configure(
    processors=[
        structlog.stdlib.filter_by_level,
        structlog.stdlib.add_logger_name,
        structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
        structlog.stdlib.PositionalArgumentsFormatter(),
        structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt='iso'),
        structlog.processors.StackInfoRenderer(),
        structlog.processors.format_exc_info,
        structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
    ],
    wrapper_class=structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger,
    context_class=dict,
)

log = structlog.get_logger()
log.info('request_completed', request_id='abc123', duration_ms=45)

# === Contextual logging with LoggerAdapter ===
class RequestLogger(logging.LoggerAdapter):
    def process(self, msg, kwargs):
        kwargs['extra'] = {**self.extra, **(kwargs.get('extra') or {})}
        return msg, kwargs

logger = RequestLogger(logging.getLogger(__name__), {'request_id': 'abc123'})
logger.info('processing')  # automatically includes request_id

sections.title: 3. OpenTelemetry — Traces, Metrics, Logs
sections.description:
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the industry standard for observability. It unifies traces, metrics, and logs into a single signal. At FAANG, every Python service emits OTel telemetry.

Traces: End-to-end request journey with spans (individual operations). Metrics: Counters, histograms, gauges. Logs: Structured log records with trace/span IDs attached.

Context propagation: OTel propagates trace context across service boundaries via HTTP headers (W3C TraceContext). This enables distributed tracing across Python, Go, Java, and C++ services.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design the observability stack for a 20-service Python microservice architecture' — OTel SDK in every service for traces + metrics + logs. OTel Collector as the agent (runs on every node, processes telemetry). Backend: Prometheus (metrics), Jaeger/Tempo (traces), Loki/Elasticsearch (logs). Alerts on SLO violations based on metrics.
sections.codeBlock:
# === OpenTelemetry setup ===
# pip install opentelemetry-distro opentelemetry-exporter-otlp
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter

# Initialize tracer provider
trace.set_tracer_provider(TracerProvider())
tracer = trace.get_tracer(__name__)

# Export traces to OTel Collector
span_processor = BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint='http://otel-collector:4317'))
trace.get_tracer_provider().add_span_processor(span_processor)

# Create spans (automatic via instrumentation, or manual)
with tracer.start_as_current_span('process_request') as span:
    span.set_attribute('request_id', 'abc123')
    span.set_attribute('user_id', 42)
    span.add_event('cache_miss', {'key': 'user:42'})
    result = process_data()

# === Auto-instrumentation ===
# $ opentelemetry-instrument python myapp.py
# Injects instrumentation for Flask, Django, requests, psycopg2, etc.

# === Metrics ===
from opentelemetry import metrics
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics import MeterProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.metrics.export import PeriodicExportingMetricReader

metrics.set_meter_provider(MeterProvider([
    PeriodicExportingMetricReader(OTLPMetricExporter(endpoint='http://otel-collector:4317'))
]))
meter = metrics.get_meter(__name__)

request_counter = meter.create_counter(
    'http.requests.total',
    description='Total HTTP requests',
    unit='1',
)
request_counter.add(1, {'endpoint': '/api/users', 'status': '200'})

# === Context propagation ===
# W3C TraceContext headers are automatically injected into outgoing requests
# by OTel instrumentation, enabling cross-service distributed tracing.

sections.title: 4. Correlation IDs and Context Propagation
sections.description:
A correlation ID ties together logs, traces, and metrics for a single request across service boundaries. Without it, debugging a request that touches 5 services requires grepping timestamps.

Generation: Generate at the ingress point (API gateway or first service). UUIDv4 is standard. Propagation: Pass via HTTP header (X-Request-ID or W3C traceparent). Use middleware to inject into log contexts automatically.

Thread-safe context: Use contextvars (Python 3.7+) for per-request context that doesn't leak across requests or threads. This replaces threading.local() for async-friendly context.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'A user reports an error but has no error message. How do you debug it?' — (1) Ask for the correlation ID from the error page/response header. (2) Search logs by correlation ID. (3) Follow the trace across all services. (4) Identify the failing span. No correlation ID? That's the first bug to fix.

sections.title: 5. PII Redaction and Log Safety
sections.description:
Logging sensitive data (PII, credentials, tokens) is a compliance violation (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA) and a security risk at FAANG. Every log pipeline must redact PII before it reaches storage.

What not to log: Passwords, API keys, SSNs, email addresses, full names, IP addresses (log anonymized hash instead), credit card numbers, auth tokens.

Redaction strategies: (1) Filter at the application layer (don't log sensitive fields). (2) Redact patterns with regex in the logging formatter. (3) Strip at the log shipper (Filebeat, Fluentd) before writing to storage. Always do (1); use (2) and (3) as defense in depth.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your log aggregation system is found to contain customer PII. What do you do?' — (1) Immediate containment: purge the PII from the log store. (2) Root cause: identify how PII entered logs (code path review). (3) Fix: add redaction filter at the application layer and the log shipper. (4) Compliance: report to DPO/legal within 72 hours (GDPR requirement). (5) Prevention: add automated PII scanning in CI for log statements.

sections.title: 6. Staff+ Observability Strategy
sections.description:
At Staff+ level, you design the observability strategy, not just configure loggers.

Three pillars: Logs (what happened), Metrics (how many/how fast), Traces (where in the stack). Missing any pillar means blind spots in production debugging.

SLO-based alerting: Define Service Level Objectives (e.g., p99 latency < 200ms). Alert when error budget is burning too fast. Use metrics for SLOs, logs for debugging, traces for pinpointing.

Sampling: At scale, storing 100% of traces is too expensive. Use head-based sampling (consistent at the trace root) or tail-based sampling (keep only interesting traces — errors, high latency). Target: 1-5% of successful requests, 100% of errors.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Your service has a p99 latency spike from 100ms to 5s every day at 2PM. How do you find the root cause?' — (1) Check dashboards: is latency correlated with error rate, throughput, or resource saturation? (2) Check traces: which span is slow? (3) Check logs: any new log patterns at 2PM? (4) Correlate with deployments: did something ship at 2PM? (5) Check dependencies: is an external service slow? (6) Fix with the right intervention: rollback, scale up, optimize query, add cache.
sections.codeBlock:
# === SLO monitoring pattern ===
# Prometheus-style metrics for SLO calculation

# Counter for total requests and error requests
TOTAL_REQUESTS = meter.create_counter('http.requests.total', unit='1')
ERROR_REQUESTS = meter.create_counter('http.requests.errors', unit='1')
LATENCY_HISTOGRAM = meter.create_histogram('http.request.duration_ms', unit='ms')

def record_request(status_code: int, duration_ms: float, endpoint: str):
    attrs = {'endpoint': endpoint}
    TOTAL_REQUESTS.add(1, attrs)
    if status_code >= 500:
        ERROR_REQUESTS.add(1, attrs)
    LATENCY_HISTOGRAM.record(duration_ms, attrs)

# SLO: 99.9% of requests complete in <200ms
# Burn rate: if error rate > 0.1% over 1 hour, alert

# === Observability checklist for new services ===
# [ ] Structured JSON logging
# [ ] Correlation ID on every log line
# [ ] OTel tracing with distributed context propagation
# [ ] RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) for every endpoint
# [ ] PII redaction filter
# [ ] Health check endpoint returning dependency status
# [ ] Graceful shutdown with log flush
# [ ] SLO definition with alerting

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Python logging module — Official docs
  • python-json-logger — JSON formatter
  • structlog — Structured logging library
  • OpenTelemetry Python — Official OTel Python docs
  • Observability Engineering by Charity Majors, Liz Fong-Jones, George Miranda

details:
Observability workflow: (1) Use structured JSON logging from day one. (2) Add correlation IDs via contextvars. (3) Instrument with OpenTelemetry for traces + metrics. (4) Define SLOs with metric-based alerting. (5) Redact PII at the application layer. (6) Use sampling to control costs. (7) Build dashboards for the four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation).

Common mistakes: Using print() for logging; not including request_id in logs; logging raw request/response bodies (PII leak); no structured format (grep is painful); not configuring log levels per environment; no alerting on log-level errors; storing 100% of traces (bankrupts the observability budget).

Staff+ expectations: Design the observability strategy for the service or org. Choose the right tools (OTel, Prometheus, structured logging) and ensure they work together. Define SLOs and burn-rate alerts. Write runbooks for common failure modes. Ensure every on-call engineer can go from alert to root cause in <15 minutes using the observability stack.
